enrichment.short_description: Apache Cassandra is a free, open-source, distributed wide-column NoSQL database built to handle massive amounts of data across many commodity servers with no single point of failure. Originally developed at Facebook by Avinash Lakshman and Prashant Malik to power inbox search, it was open-sourced in 2008 and became an Apache Software Foundation top-level project in 2010. Cassandra combines Amazon Dynamo-style distributed architecture with a Google Bigtable-style column-family data model, giving it linear scalability, tunable consistency, and near-zero downtime across data centers and cloud regions. It is not a commercial product or company; it is a community-governed open-source project maintained by contributors from companies like DataStax, Instaclustr, Apple, Netflix, and many independent developers. Cassandra is widely used for write-heavy, high-throughput workloads such as messaging systems, IoT telemetry, time-series data, product catalogs, and recommendation engines where availability and horizontal scale matter more than complex relational queries or strict ACID transactions.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source community project governed by the Apache Software Foundation (not a company)
enrichment.headquarters: Wilmington, Delaware, US (Apache Software Foundation legal home; development is fully distributed globally)
enrichment.seo_title: Apache Cassandra Review 2026: Features, Architecture and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An in-depth look at Apache Cassandra, the free open-source distributed NoSQL database: architecture, features, use cases, pros and cons, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apache Cassandra is a free, open-source distributed wide-column NoSQL database designed for massive scalability and high availability with no single point of failure. It began inside Facebook in the mid-2000s, created by engineers Avinash Lakshman (a co-author of Amazon's original Dynamo paper) and Prashant Malik to solve the company's inbox search problem, which demanded very high write throughput across billions of records. Facebook open-sourced Cassandra in July 2008, and the project entered the Apache Incubator in March 2009 before graduating to a top-level Apache Software Foundation project in February 2010. Since then it has been governed entirely as a community open-source project rather than a commercial product, meaning there is no single vendor, subscription, or company behind it, although several companies (DataStax, Instaclustr/NetApp, Apple, Netflix, and others) employ core contributors and offer commercial support or managed hosting around it. Architecturally, Cassandra borrows the peer-to-peer, masterless replication model from Amazon's Dynamo and the column-family data model from Google's Bigtable, giving every node in a cluster equal responsibility so there is no single point of failure and the system keeps serving reads and writes even during hardware failures or network partitions. Its consistency levels are tunable per query, letting engineering teams trade off consistency, availability, and latency to fit the workload. As of mid-2026 the actively maintained release line is the 5.0.x series (5.0.8 being a recent release), which backported automated repair tooling and TLS 1.3 support from the in-development 6.0 branch, while Cassandra 4.1 remains in supported use for existing deployments; a 6.0 alpha is publicly available for teams testing the next major version. Cassandra is best known for powering data platforms that need to write huge volumes of data continuously and read it back fast at global scale: messaging inboxes, IoT sensor streams, time-series metrics, fraud and security event pipelines, product catalogs, and personalization or recommendation data stores. It deliberately sacrifices some relational-database conveniences, such as complex joins, foreign keys, and full ACID multi-row transactions, in exchange for linear horizontal scalability, multi-datacenter replication out of the box, and operational resilience. Because it is free and open source, there is no vendor lock-in or licensing cost, but teams do need real operational expertise to run it well, from correct data modeling around partition keys to compaction strategy tuning and cluster capacity planning. For directory and SEO research purposes, it is important to treat Cassandra as infrastructure software and an Apache Software Foundation project rather than a SaaS company with funding rounds, a headquarters staff, or a sales team; any pricing, support, or managed-service costs come from third-party vendors that host or support Cassandra, not from the Cassandra project itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Architecture and History
paragraphs: Cassandra's defining trait is its masterless, peer-to-peer architecture: every node in a cluster is identical and can accept both reads and writes, and data is automatically partitioned and replicated across nodes using consistent hashing. This design, inherited from Amazon's internal Dynamo system, means there is no single point of failure and no need for manual failover when a node goes down. On top of that distributed foundation, Cassandra layers a wide-column data model similar to Google's Bigtable, where each row can have a flexible, sparse set of columns grouped into column families (tables), which makes it well suited to semi-structured, high-velocity data.

The project traces back to Facebook, where engineers Avinash Lakshman and Prashant Malik built it to solve the inbox search problem: search had to scale to hundreds of millions of users and billions of writes per day, something existing relational systems of the era could not handle affordably. Facebook released the code as open source in July 2008, and the Apache Software Foundation took it in as an incubator project in 2009, promoting it to a full top-level project in 2010. Since then, Cassandra has been developed entirely in the open by a community of contributors rather than by a single vendor, with major users including Apple, Netflix, Instagram, and thousands of smaller engineering teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Where Cassandra Fits and What It Trades Off
paragraphs: Cassandra is optimized for workloads that need to ingest enormous volumes of writes continuously, remain available during hardware or network failures, and scale horizontally simply by adding more commodity nodes rather than buying bigger servers. This makes it a common choice for time-series and IoT telemetry, messaging and notification systems, product catalogs, session stores, and any system where uptime and write throughput matter more than complex ad-hoc queries. Its tunable consistency model lets teams choose, on a per-query basis, how many replicas must acknowledge a read or write, trading off strict consistency for lower latency and higher availability as needed.

What Cassandra deliberately does not offer is the flexibility of a general-purpose relational database: there are no multi-table joins, foreign key constraints, or full multi-row ACID transactions in the traditional sense, and effective use requires modeling tables around known query patterns rather than normalizing data the way a relational schema would. Running Cassandra well in production also demands genuine operational know-how around partition key design, compaction strategy, repair scheduling, and capacity planning, which is why many teams use managed Cassandra services or commercial support from companies like DataStax or Instaclustr rather than operating raw open-source clusters themselves.

features.title: Masterless, peer-to-peer architecture
features.description: Every node in a Cassandra cluster is equal and can serve reads and writes, eliminating single points of failure and simplifying horizontal scaling.

features.description: Throughput and storage capacity scale close to linearly by adding more nodes, without downtime or complex resharding operations.

features.title: Tunable consistency
features.description: Reads and writes can specify consistency levels per query, letting teams balance consistency, latency, and availability for each use case.

features.title: Multi-datacenter and multi-cloud replication
features.description: Built-in replication strategies let clusters span multiple data centers or cloud regions for disaster recovery and low-latency global access.

features.description: Tables support sparse, flexible columns per row, suited to semi-structured and rapidly evolving data models.

features.title: CQL (Cassandra Query Language)
features.description: A SQL-like query language makes Cassandra approachable for teams familiar with relational syntax, despite the underlying non-relational model.

features.title: High write throughput
features.description: Cassandra's log-structured storage engine (memtables plus SSTables) is optimized for extremely fast, append-heavy writes at massive scale.

features.title: Fully open source, no vendor lock-in
features.description: Licensed under Apache License 2.0 with governance by the Apache Software Foundation, so there is no proprietary license fee or single controlling vendor.

faqs.question: Is Apache Cassandra free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cassandra is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no licensing fees. Costs only arise from the infrastructure you run it on or from optional commercial support and managed hosting services offered by third-party vendors.

faqs.question: Who owns or controls Apache Cassandra?
faqs.answer: No single company owns Cassandra. It is governed by the Apache Software Foundation as a community-driven open-source project. Companies such as DataStax, Instaclustr (part of NetApp), and Apple employ core contributors and offer commercial products around it, but the project itself is vendor-neutral.

faqs.question: What is the current stable version of Cassandra?
faqs.answer: As of mid-2026 the actively supported release line is the 5.0.x series, with 5.0.8 among the recent updates, while the 4.1 series remains supported for existing deployments. A 6.0 alpha is publicly available for teams evaluating the next major release.

faqs.question: What is Cassandra best used for?
faqs.answer: Cassandra excels at write-heavy, high-availability workloads such as time-series and IoT data, messaging and notification systems, product catalogs, session storage, and any application that needs to stay online and accept writes even during infrastructure failures.

faqs.question: How is Cassandra different from a relational database like PostgreSQL or MySQL?
faqs.answer: Cassandra is a distributed, masterless, wide-column NoSQL store designed for horizontal scale and availability, whereas relational databases like PostgreSQL emphasize strong consistency, complex joins, and full ACID transactions on a typically vertically-scaled or primary-replica architecture.

faqs.question: Does Cassandra support SQL?
faqs.answer: Not standard SQL, but it provides CQL (Cassandra Query Language), a SQL-inspired language for defining schemas and querying data, designed specifically around Cassandra's distributed data model.

faqs.question: Can Cassandra run across multiple cloud providers or regions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cassandra has built-in support for multi-datacenter replication, which teams commonly use to run clusters across multiple cloud regions or even multiple cloud providers for redundancy and low-latency global access.

faqs.question: Do I need Cassandra-specific expertise to run it in production?
faqs.answer: Generally yes. Effective Cassandra usage requires careful data modeling around partition keys and query patterns, along with operational knowledge of compaction, repair, and capacity planning, which is why many organizations use managed Cassandra services or commercial support rather than self-operating clusters.

enrichment.short_description: Castle is an account security and fraud prevention platform that helps online businesses stop bots, account takeovers, fake account creation, and other forms of account abuse in real time. Founded in 2015 in Malmo, Sweden by Sebastian Wallin and Johan Brissmyr, the company later moved to the San Francisco Bay Area to join Y Combinator's Winter 2016 batch and has since raised roughly $13.7 million from investors including Index Ventures, First Round Capital, and Y Combinator. Castle analyzes signals like device fingerprinting, behavioral biometrics, IP and proxy data, and email intelligence to produce real-time risk scores, typically responding within around 100 milliseconds, and it can be deployed both at the network edge and inside application code for layered protection. Customers use Castle to detect and block account takeover attempts, fake sign-ups, multi-accounting abuse, SMS pumping fraud, and API abuse, with notable customers including Atlassian, Rockstar Games, Rakuten, Canva, and Framer. Pricing is usage-based, starting with a free evaluation tier and scaling through a self-serve Pro plan into custom Enterprise agreements.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: An in-depth review of Castle, the account security and fraud detection platform: features, pricing, pros and cons, and how it stops bots and account takeovers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Castle is an account security and fraud detection platform built to help online businesses stop bots, account takeovers, and other forms of account abuse before they cause damage. The company was founded in 2015 by Sebastian Wallin and Johan Brissmyr in Malmo, Sweden, and the founders relocated to the San Francisco Bay Area in 2016 to join Y Combinator's Winter 2016 cohort. Since then Castle has raised approximately $13.7 million in funding, including a $9.2 million Series A led by Index Ventures with participation from First Round Capital, F-Prime Capital Partners, Y Combinator, and individual angel investors. The company positions itself as bringing fraud-detection technology previously reserved for banks and large financial institutions to online businesses of any size. Technically, Castle takes a dual-layer approach: it can deploy detection logic at the network edge, including a direct integration with Cloudflare, to block obviously malicious traffic before it ever reaches an application's backend, while also embedding lightweight analytics inside the application itself to catch more sophisticated, human-like abuse that only reveals itself through behavioral signals. Castle's risk engine draws on device fingerprinting (claimed around 99.5 percent accuracy), keystroke and behavioral biometrics, browsing history signals, IP geolocation and proxy/VPN detection, and email reputation intelligence, combining hundreds of signals into Bot Score and Abuse Score outputs that applications can act on with an allow, challenge, or deny decision, typically within about 100 milliseconds. The platform retains historical event data, up to 18 months on Enterprise plans, enabling network-level analysis that can connect related fraudulent accounts and devices across a customer's user base. Common use cases include preventing fake account creation during sign-up, detecting and stopping account takeover attempts, blocking multi-accounting and promo abuse, catching SMS pumping fraud, preventing content and platform abuse, and defending APIs against automated attacks. Castle's customer base includes recognizable names such as Atlassian, Rockstar Games, Rakuten, Canva, and Framer, and the company says it processes billions of requests per month across its customer base. Pricing follows a transparent, usage-based model: a free tier allows evaluation with up to 1,000 API calls a month, a self-serve Pro plan starts at $200 per month for the first 100,000 calls with additional calls billed at $0.002 each, and Enterprise plans start around $4,000 per month with custom Monthly Tracked User or volume-based pricing, extended data retention, dedicated Slack support, and SLA guarantees. Because Castle competes in the same space as larger platforms like Arkose Labs, DataDome, and Sift, its main differentiators are developer-friendly integration, transparent linear pricing with no long-term contract minimums on lower tiers, and the combined edge-plus-in-app detection architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Castle Detects and Stops Fraud
paragraphs: Castle's core value proposition is combining two layers of defense: edge-level filtering that intercepts obviously automated or malicious traffic before it reaches a customer's servers, and in-application analytics that continue monitoring user behavior after a session begins, where more sophisticated attacks tend to hide. This dual-layer model, including a direct Cloudflare integration for edge deployment, lets Castle catch both blunt bot traffic and more nuanced account takeover attempts carried out by real people using stolen credentials or session hijacking techniques.

Underlying both layers is a risk-scoring engine that ingests device fingerprints, behavioral biometrics such as typing patterns and mouse movement, IP geolocation and proxy or VPN detection, and email reputation data, producing real-time Bot Score and Abuse Score outputs that typically resolve within about 100 milliseconds. Development teams can wire these scores into policy rules, ranging from silently allowing a request to challenging it with additional verification to denying it outright, and Castle retains historical event data, up to 18 months on higher-tier plans, to support deeper network analysis that can surface clusters of related fraudulent accounts or devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Structure and Who Castle Is For
paragraphs: Castle markets its pricing as transparent and linearly scaling, in contrast to fraud-prevention vendors that require lengthy sales cycles for any pricing information. The free tier, capped at 1,000 API calls a month, exists purely for evaluation before going live. The Pro tier is self-serve at $200 per month for the first 100,000 calls, with overages billed at $0.002 per additional call and no long-term contractual minimums, making it accessible to startups and mid-sized companies that want to get started without an enterprise sales process. Larger organizations that need custom Monthly Tracked User pricing, extended data retention up to 18 months, dedicated Slack channels, and formal SLAs move into Enterprise agreements that start around $4,000 per month.

Given its customer roster, which includes consumer platforms like Canva and Rockstar Games alongside marketplaces like Rakuten, Castle tends to fit best for consumer-facing digital products and marketplaces with meaningful sign-up and login volume, where account takeover, fake account creation, and promo or content abuse are ongoing operational problems. Its developer-first integration model and self-serve pricing tier make it a reasonable starting point for engineering teams that want to add fraud detection without an immediate enterprise procurement process, while its Enterprise tier and edge integrations serve larger platforms that need deeper historical analysis and guaranteed support.

faqs.answer: Castle is an account security and fraud detection platform that analyzes signup, login, and in-app behavior in real time to detect and stop bots, account takeovers, fake account creation, and other forms of account abuse.

faqs.answer: Castle offers a free tier for evaluation (up to 1,000 calls/month), a self-serve Pro plan at $200/month for the first 100,000 calls plus $0.002 per additional call, and custom Enterprise pricing starting around $4,000/month with volume or Monthly Tracked User based billing.

faqs.question: When was Castle founded and by whom?
faqs.answer: Castle was founded in 2015 in Malmo, Sweden by Sebastian Wallin and Johan Brissmyr. The founders relocated to the San Francisco Bay Area in 2016 to join Y Combinator's Winter 2016 batch.

faqs.question: How fast does Castle return a risk decision?
faqs.answer: Castle typically returns real-time risk assessments in around 100 milliseconds, making it usable inline in signup, login, and checkout flows without noticeably slowing the user experience.

faqs.question: Does Castle work with Cloudflare?
faqs.answer: Yes. Castle offers a direct integration for Cloudflare that allows detection to run at the network edge in addition to in-application analytics, giving customers a combined edge-plus-in-app defense layer.

faqs.question: Who uses Castle?
faqs.answer: Castle's customers include consumer and enterprise platforms such as Atlassian, Rockstar Games, Rakuten, Canva, and Framer, spanning gaming, e-commerce, productivity, and design software companies.

faqs.answer: Castle is used to detect and prevent account takeovers, fake account creation, multi-accounting and promo abuse, SMS pumping fraud, content abuse, and automated API abuse.

enrichment.short_description: Catalyst (catalyst.io) is a customer success platform built to help customer success and revenue teams centralize customer data, track account health, and drive retention and expansion. Important distinction: this Catalyst is an entirely different product from Catalyst by Zoho, the serverless backend-as-a-service platform covered separately in this directory; the two share only a name and have no corporate or technical relationship. This Catalyst was founded in 2017 in New York City by brothers Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu and grew into a Series C company backed by roughly $63.4 million in funding from investors including Accel, Spark Capital, and Ludlow Ventures. In a significant development, Catalyst Software has merged with Totango, another prominent customer success platform, combining the two companies' technology to offer what they describe as more complete customer growth solutions for customer success and sales organizations. The platform integrates with a company's existing tool stack to give a single, unified view of customer health, usage, and engagement, enabling customer success managers to proactively intervene before churn happens rather than reacting after the fact. Notable customers cited by the company include SAP, GitHub, Schneider Electric, Dynatrace, Aircall, Braze, Fivetran, and Heap. Pricing is not publicly published; prospective customers must request a demo to receive a quote.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Catalyst, found at catalyst.io, is a customer success platform designed to help customer success, account management, and revenue teams centralize customer data and proactively manage account health to reduce churn and grow revenue. It is important to distinguish this Catalyst clearly from Catalyst by Zoho, a completely separate serverless backend-as-a-service and app development platform from Zoho Corporation; despite sharing the same name, these are unrelated products from unrelated companies serving entirely different purposes, one for customer success teams and one for software developers. This Catalyst was founded in 2017 in New York City by brothers Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu, who built what the company describes as the most intuitive customer success platform on the market. Over its history the company raised approximately $63.4 million across funding rounds up to a Series C, from investors including Accel, Spark Capital, and Ludlow Ventures, positioning it among the more well-funded standalone customer success platforms. A major development in the company's trajectory was its merger with Totango, another established customer success platform vendor; together, the combined company positions itself as offering more powerful, unified customer growth solutions for customer success and sales teams, blending Catalyst's product with Totango's technology and customer base. Functionally, Catalyst is built around the idea of giving customer success managers a single, centralized view of every account by integrating with the other tools already in a company's stack, such as CRM, product analytics, support, and billing systems, so that usage trends, support tickets, health scores, and other signals live in one place rather than scattered across disconnected systems. This lets CS teams move from reactive account management to proactive intervention, for example receiving automated alerts when a customer stops using features considered critical to their long-term retention, so a customer success manager can reach out before the account is at serious churn risk. The platform serves customer success and revenue teams at business-to-business software companies, and its publicly cited customer list includes recognizable enterprise and mid-market names such as SAP, GitHub, Schneider Electric, Dynatrace, and Aircall, alongside revenue-focused brands like Braze, Fivetran, and Heap. Catalyst does not publish self-serve pricing tiers or a price list on its marketing site; like most enterprise customer success software, prospective customers must request a demo and go through a sales conversation to receive a customized quote, which is typical for B2B software in this category where pricing usually scales with number of managed accounts, users, or contract value. Because of the ambiguity created by other products also using the Catalyst name, most notably Zoho's developer platform, anyone researching this product should specifically look for catalyst.io or references to the Totango merger to confirm they are looking at the correct customer success company rather than the unrelated backend-as-a-service platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Catalyst Is (and Is Not)
paragraphs: Catalyst, accessible at catalyst.io, is a customer success platform, a category of B2B software used by customer success and account management teams to monitor account health, track product usage, and manage renewal and expansion workflows. It should not be confused with Catalyst by Zoho, an unrelated serverless backend-as-a-service and full-stack app development platform built by Zoho Corporation for software developers. The two products happen to share a name but were built by entirely different companies, for entirely different audiences, with no technical or corporate connection between them. Buyers and researchers should confirm which Catalyst they mean before evaluating features or pricing, since search results for the plain term Catalyst frequently surface both products interchangeably.

This Catalyst was founded in 2017 by brothers Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu in New York City and grew into a venture-backed company that raised roughly $63.4 million through a Series C round, with investors including Accel, Spark Capital, and Ludlow Ventures. The company's core pitch was being the most intuitive customer success platform on the market, aimed at making it easier for CS teams to see account health and usage signals without needing heavy technical configuration.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Totango Merger and How Teams Use Catalyst
paragraphs: A defining recent event for the company is its merger with Totango, another well-known customer success platform vendor. Rather than operating as two competing products, the combined organization now positions itself as delivering more complete customer growth solutions by pairing Catalyst's product experience with Totango's technology and customer relationships, aimed at helping CS and sales teams drive retention and expansion together rather than working from separate systems.

In day-to-day use, Catalyst connects to the other tools a company already relies on, such as CRM, support, billing, and product analytics platforms, consolidating that data into a single customer view. Customer success managers use this view to build account health scores, track engagement trends, and receive proactive alerts, for instance when a customer's usage of a feature considered critical to their success starts declining, giving the team a chance to intervene before the account churns. This proactive, centralized approach is what the company's customer roster, including SAP, GitHub, Schneider Electric, Dynatrace, Aircall, Braze, Fivetran, and Heap, is reported to rely on for managing large books of business.

faqs.question: Is Catalyst (catalyst.io) the same as Catalyst by Zoho?
faqs.answer: No. Catalyst at catalyst.io is a customer success platform for CS and revenue teams, founded in 2017 by Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu. Catalyst by Zoho is a completely separate serverless backend-as-a-service platform built by Zoho Corporation for software developers. The two products are unrelated aside from sharing a name.

faqs.question: What does Catalyst (the customer success platform) actually do?
faqs.answer: It centralizes customer data from a company's existing tools into a single account health view, and gives customer success teams proactive alerts and workflows to reduce churn and drive renewal and expansion.

faqs.question: Who founded Catalyst and when?
faqs.answer: Catalyst was founded in 2017 in New York City by brothers Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu.

faqs.question: Has Catalyst been acquired or merged with another company?
faqs.answer: Yes. Catalyst Software has merged with Totango, another established customer success platform, and the combined company now markets itself as offering unified customer growth solutions.

faqs.question: How much does Catalyst cost?
faqs.answer: Catalyst does not publish pricing publicly. Prospective customers need to request a demo to receive a customized quote, which is standard practice for enterprise customer success software.

faqs.question: Who are Catalyst's typical customers?
faqs.answer: Catalyst serves customer success and revenue teams primarily at B2B software companies, with publicly referenced customers including SAP, GitHub, Schneider Electric, Dynatrace, Aircall, Braze, Fivetran, and Heap.

faqs.question: How much funding has Catalyst raised?
faqs.answer: Catalyst raised approximately $63.4 million in venture funding through a Series C round, with investors including Accel, Spark Capital, and Ludlow Ventures.

faqs.question: Is Catalyst suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Catalyst is generally built for companies with dedicated customer success organizations and a meaningful base of paying accounts to manage, making it more suited to mid-market and enterprise B2B companies than very small businesses.

enrichment.short_description: Catalyst by Zoho is Zoho Corporation's serverless, full-stack backend-as-a-service platform for building and hosting web and mobile applications without managing infrastructure. It is a completely different product from the standalone customer success platform simply called Catalyst (catalyst.io) also covered in this directory; the two share only a name, with no relationship between the two companies or products. Zoho first announced Catalyst publicly on October 17, 2019 as part of an initiative to open up the same internal infrastructure that powers Zoho's own suite of business applications, and the platform reached broader general availability in the years following. Catalyst provides Function-as-a-Service compute in Java and Node.js across five function types (basic I/O, advanced I/O, event functions, scheduled/periodic functions, and integration functions), alongside managed backend services like a relational data store, file storage, caching, authentication, and Zia-powered AI and ML capabilities such as OCR and object detection. Pricing follows a free-tier-forever model (more than 70 components included at no cost every month) plus a pay-as-you-go option with a $5 monthly minimum once free-tier limits are exceeded, and an optional subscription model for teams that prefer predictable monthly budgeting, starting from example plans around $25 to $30 per month. As part of Zoho Corporation, a large, privately held, profitable software company founded in 1996, Catalyst benefits from deep integration with the broader Zoho ecosystem of 55-plus business applications.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Catalyst by Zoho is a serverless, full-stack backend-as-a-service and application development platform built by Zoho Corporation, designed to let developers build, host, and scale web and mobile applications without provisioning or managing their own servers. It is important to note this is an entirely different product from the standalone Catalyst customer success platform at catalyst.io, which is built by an unrelated company for customer success teams rather than developers; the shared name is coincidental and the two products have no technical or business relationship. Zoho publicly announced Catalyst on October 17, 2019, describing it as opening up the same internal serverless infrastructure that already powers Zoho's own suite of more than 55 business applications, such as Zoho CRM, Zoho Books, and Zoho Mail, so that outside developers could build on the same foundation. The platform reached general availability in the years following its initial announcement, with Zoho continuing to expand its capabilities through the early 2020s. At its core, Catalyst offers Function-as-a-Service compute supporting Java and Node.js, organized into five distinct function types: Basic I/O Functions for straightforward request-response logic, Advanced I/O Functions for streaming and parsing large payloads, Event Functions and Event Listeners triggered by specific application or platform events, periodic functions for scheduled or cron-style tasks, and Integration Functions built specifically to connect with other Zoho services. Beyond compute, Catalyst bundles a set of managed backend services that developers would otherwise need to stitch together themselves, including a relational Data Store, file storage, a caching layer, user authentication, and a workflow orchestration feature called Circuits for coordinating multi-step application logic. It also exposes Zia, Zoho's AI engine, giving developers access to prebuilt AI and machine learning capabilities such as optical character recognition and object detection without training their own models, plus SDKs for iOS, Android, and web to build fully custom client applications on top of the platform. On pricing, Catalyst uses a free-tier-forever model that includes more than 70 components at no cost every month, such as a documented allotment of serverless function compute (measured in GB-seconds), a couple of gigabytes of Data Store storage, and a batch of free cache requests, refreshed automatically each billing cycle. First-time users additionally receive a one-time $250 credit of free resources. Once free-tier limits are exceeded, billing defaults to a postpaid pay-as-you-go model with a $5 monthly minimum, charging only for resources actually consumed with no separate subscription fee; alternatively, teams that prefer predictable budgeting can choose a subscription model, where Zoho cites an example Basic plan around $25 per month that effectively becomes $30 per month worth of usage because Zoho adds an extra $5 of resources on top of the selected plan at no additional charge. Elastic auto-scaling, end-to-end security around function and service access, and cost efficiency (paying only for actual compute time rather than idle server capacity) are among Catalyst's core selling points, and because it is built and operated by Zoho Corporation, a large, profitable, privately held software company founded in 1996 with dual headquarters in Chennai, India and Austin, Texas, Catalyst benefits from tight integration with the rest of Zoho's application ecosystem alongside enterprise-grade infrastructure reliability.

long_form_blocks.heading: A Distinct Product From the Standalone Catalyst
paragraphs: Before evaluating features, it is worth stating clearly: Catalyst by Zoho is not the same product as Catalyst at catalyst.io, a standalone customer success platform founded in 2017 by Edward Chiu and Kevin Chiu that has since merged with Totango. Catalyst by Zoho is a developer-focused serverless application platform built by Zoho Corporation, an entirely different company, for an entirely different audience of software engineers rather than customer success teams. The two products happen to share the Catalyst name coincidentally, and anyone researching one should be careful not to conflate its features, pricing, or company background with the other.

long_form_blocks.heading: Platform Capabilities and Pricing Model
paragraphs: Catalyst's central offering is Function-as-a-Service compute in Java and Node.js, split across five function types covering everything from simple request handling to large data streaming, event-driven triggers, scheduled tasks, and integrations with other Zoho products. Around that compute layer, Zoho bundles the managed backend services most applications need out of the box, including a relational Data Store, file storage, caching, authentication, and a workflow orchestration tool called Circuits, alongside access to Zia, Zoho's AI engine, for capabilities like OCR and object detection without building custom ML infrastructure. SDKs for iOS, Android, and web let developers build fully custom client applications on top of these backend services.

Pricing is structured to be accessible for small projects while scaling to production workloads. The free tier includes more than 70 components at no cost every month, refreshed automatically, plus a one-time $250 credit for first-time users. After exceeding free-tier limits, Catalyst defaults to postpaid pay-as-you-go billing with a $5 monthly minimum and no separate subscription charge, so cost tracks actual usage. Teams that prefer predictable monthly budgeting can instead opt into a subscription model, where Zoho's own example cites a Basic plan around $25 per month, with Zoho adding an extra $5 of resource allowance on top at no additional cost. This combination of a genuinely useful free tier, usage-based pricing, and Zoho's broader ecosystem integration makes Catalyst a practical option for teams already using other Zoho products, or for developers who want a serverless platform without negotiating enterprise cloud contracts.

faqs.answer: It is used to build and host full-stack web and mobile applications without managing servers, providing serverless compute (functions), a managed database, file storage, caching, authentication, AI/ML services, and workflow orchestration in one platform.

enrichment.short_description: CatchDoms is an expired and pending-delete domain discovery platform built for domain investors, SEO professionals, and entrepreneurs looking to find valuable dropped or auctioned domains. It aggregates listings from roughly 16-20 domain auction platforms, including GoDaddy, Dynadot, DropCatch, SnapNames, Catched, and Gname, alongside a database of over 100,000 aged ccTLD domains available to register at standard price, adding tens of thousands of newly discovered domains every day. Every domain is automatically enriched with SEO and authority metrics such as Trust Flow, Citation Flow, Domain Authority, backlink counts, referring domains, and Wayback Machine history, so buyers can gauge a domain's value before bidding rather than manually checking each metric across separate tools. CatchDoms was built and is operated by a solo, bootstrapped founder, Samir Belabbes, who previously spent over a decade in SEO and web development and built the tool to solve his own workflow problem of checking a dozen auction platforms and multiple SEO tools every morning. Beyond the web interface, CatchDoms offers a REST API and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, letting developers and AI assistants like Claude or Cursor query expired-domain data programmatically or conversationally. Pricing includes a free tier showing limited visible domains, and a paid Pro plan around EUR 39 per month billed annually (EUR 89 per month billed monthly, or EUR 468 per year), with higher Authority and Authority plus tiers adding features like the aged-ccTLD regfee database and founder access.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CatchDoms is an expired-domain intelligence platform designed to help domain investors, SEO professionals, and entrepreneurs find valuable dropped, deleted, or auctioned domain names without manually checking dozens of separate auction sites and SEO tools every day. The platform aggregates domain listings from a wide range of auction and registrar platforms, sources cited across its own materials range from roughly ten to twenty platforms including GoDaddy, Dynadot, DropCatch, SnapNames, Catched, Gname, BloomUp, UKBackorder, and DomainLore, and also maintains a database of more than 100,000 aged ccTLD domains that are available to register directly at standard registration price rather than through an auction. The service reports adding tens of thousands of newly discovered domains each day, with a total live inventory in the hundreds of thousands, and every domain listing is automatically enriched with SEO and authority signals such as Trust Flow and Citation Flow (Majestic metrics), Domain Authority, backlink counts, referring domain counts, category classification, domain age, and historical Wayback Machine snapshots, so a buyer can assess whether a domain is worth bidding on without pulling each metric manually from separate tools like Majestic or Ahrefs. CatchDoms was built and continues to be run by a solo, bootstrapped founder, Samir Belabbes, who has described spending over a decade in SEO and web development before building the tool to solve his own recurring problem as a domain investor: checking around a dozen different auction platforms each morning, running each candidate domain through Majestic, and then manually digging through the Wayback Machine to check its history, a workflow he consolidated into a single searchable platform with built-in filtering by domain length, TLD, language, technical stack, and category. Because the product is described publicly as a one-person, pre-seed, bootstrapped SaaS business rather than a venture-backed company, exact founding date, headquarters, and team size are not formally published; available evidence, including the site's own blog and developer write-ups, points to the platform being a relatively recent launch, likely dating to around 2024, though an exact founding date could not be independently confirmed and should be treated as an estimate rather than a verified fact. Beyond its web search interface, CatchDoms exposes a REST API for developers who want to integrate expired-domain data into their own scripts, spreadsheets, or acquisition pipelines, as well as a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that lets AI coding assistants and agents such as Claude, Cursor, and Windsurf query expired-domain data conversationally, for example asking an AI assistant to find recently dropped domains in a specific niche with strong backlink profiles. On pricing, CatchDoms uses a freemium model: the free tier shows a limited number of visible domains (reported around ten) as a preview of the database, while the paid Pro plan unlocks full access to the live database and costs roughly EUR 39 per month when billed annually (equivalent to EUR 468 per year), or EUR 89 per month on a month-to-month basis. Above Pro, the platform also offers Authority and Authority plus tiers; Authority is described as adding access to the aged-ccTLD regfee database of domains registrable at standard price, a quarterly call with the founder, and early access to an upcoming post-auction monitoring feature, while exact public pricing for the Authority and Authority plus tiers was not confirmed in available sources at the time of this profile and should not be assumed without checking the current pricing page directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Problem CatchDoms Solves
paragraphs: Domain investors and SEO professionals hunting for valuable expired or dropped domains have traditionally had to check many separate auction platforms manually every day, since no single registrar or marketplace lists all domains coming up for auction or deletion. On top of that, evaluating whether a given domain is actually worth bidding on typically requires pulling authority and backlink metrics from tools like Majestic or Ahrefs and manually reviewing a domain's history through the Wayback Machine, a slow, repetitive process when done across dozens of candidate domains a day. CatchDoms consolidates this workflow into a single searchable platform, aggregating listings from a wide set of auction and registrar sources and automatically attaching Trust Flow, Citation Flow, Domain Authority, backlink data, and Wayback history to each domain, so a user can filter and evaluate opportunities in one place rather than juggling a dozen browser tabs and separate subscriptions.

The platform was built by Samir Belabbes, who describes himself as a solo founder running CatchDoms as a bootstrapped, pre-seed SaaS business after more than ten years working in SEO and web development. According to his own account of building the product, the motivation came directly from his personal frustration as a domain investor checking roughly a dozen auction platforms every morning and manually cross-referencing SEO metrics and historical snapshots for each promising domain, a workflow CatchDoms was built specifically to automate.

long_form_blocks.heading: API, MCP Access, and Pricing Structure
paragraphs: Beyond its web-based search interface, CatchDoms exposes both a REST API and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, a notable and relatively unusual feature for a domain tool. The REST API lets developers pull expired-domain data programmatically into their own tools, scripts, or acquisition pipelines, while the MCP server allows AI coding assistants such as Claude, Cursor, and Windsurf to query the same expired-domain dataset conversationally, effectively letting a user ask an AI assistant to search for domains matching specific criteria without leaving their coding environment.

CatchDoms follows a freemium pricing structure. The free tier gives a limited preview of visible domains from the live database, while the Pro plan unlocks full access for roughly EUR 39 per month when billed annually (EUR 468 per year total) or EUR 89 per month billed monthly. Above Pro, the platform advertises Authority and Authority plus tiers aimed at more serious domain investors, with Authority reported to add access to a database of aged ccTLD domains registrable at standard price, a recurring call with the founder, and early access to features still in development, such as post-auction monitoring; specific published pricing for these higher tiers was not consistently available in public sources, so prospective buyers should confirm current numbers directly on the CatchDoms pricing page.

enrichment.short_description: CatchJS is a lightweight JavaScript error tracking, web performance monitoring, and privacy-friendly analytics tool aimed at developers who want a simple, fast alternative to heavier monitoring platforms. Founded in 2018 and based in Trondheim, Norway, CatchJS was built around the philosophy that good developer tools lead to higher-quality products, and it differentiates itself primarily through its footprint: the tracking script is reported to be under 1.8KB after compression, described by the company as up to 20 times smaller than competing error-tracking scripts. Once a single script tag is added to a site, CatchJS automatically captures unhandled JavaScript errors with full stack traces and surrounding context, while also measuring real-user performance metrics such as Largest Contentful Paint, Cumulative Layout Shift, and First Input Delay, alongside simple, privacy-respecting page analytics that avoid invasive user tracking. It is distributed both as a standalone JavaScript snippet for any website and as a dedicated Shopify app, Error Tracking with CatchJS, making it accessible to e-commerce store owners who are not full-time developers. Pricing is tiered by monthly error volume and monitored URL count, starting with a Development plan intended for staging and testing use, scaling to a Production plan for live sites, and topping out with an Enterprise plan for high-traffic applications, all backed by a 14-day free trial.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CatchJS is a JavaScript error tracking and web performance monitoring tool built for developers who want visibility into client-side errors and page performance without adopting a heavy, complex observability platform. The company was founded in 2018 (with public launch activity dated around September 26, 2018) and is based in Trondheim, Norway, positioning itself around the belief that good developer tools lead directly to higher-quality products for end users. CatchJS's standout technical characteristic is its footprint: the tracking script is reported to be less than 1.8 kilobytes after compression, which the company describes as up to 20 times smaller than competing JavaScript error-tracking scripts, a meaningful differentiator for teams concerned about page-weight and Core Web Vitals impact from third-party monitoring tags. Setup is intentionally minimal, requiring only a single script tag to be added to a page, after which CatchJS begins automatically capturing unhandled JavaScript errors along with full stack traces and surrounding execution context so developers can diagnose issues without needing to reproduce them manually. Beyond error capture, CatchJS also measures real-user performance metrics tied to user experience and search ranking signals, including Largest Contentful Paint, Cumulative Layout Shift, and First Input Delay, giving teams insight into how real visitors experience page load and interactivity rather than relying solely on synthetic lab testing. The product additionally includes simple, privacy-respecting page analytics, tracking pageviews and time-on-page without the invasive user-level tracking associated with larger analytics suites, appealing to teams that want basic usage insight without compromising visitor privacy or requiring cookie consent complexity. CatchJS is available both as a standalone JavaScript snippet that can be dropped into any website regardless of framework, and as a dedicated Shopify app called Error Tracking with CatchJS, listed in the Shopify App Store, which extends its reach to e-commerce store owners and Shopify theme developers who want to know when a storefront's JavaScript breaks and potentially disrupts checkout or browsing. Pricing is structured around three tiers that scale by monthly logged error volume, log API calls, and the number of URLs monitored for performance and pageview tracking. The Development plan, aimed at staging or lower-traffic use, starts at $44 per month billed annually or $49 per month billed monthly, including 1,000 logged errors per month and performance tracking for 100 URLs. The Production plan, aimed at live sites, runs $79 per month annually or $89 per month monthly, with 100,000 logged errors per month and performance tracking across 1,000 URLs. The top Enterprise plan, marked as the most popular option in the company's own pricing page, costs $449 per month annually or $499 per month monthly, supporting up to 1,000,000 logged errors per month, tracking across 10,000 URLs, and coverage for up to 25 domains plus unlimited subdomains. All plans include email notifications, error screenshots, click-trail context leading up to an error, and priority support, and every tier comes with a 14-day free trial requiring no long-term commitment to evaluate.

long_form_blocks.heading: What CatchJS Does
paragraphs: CatchJS focuses on three connected problems web developers face after shipping code to production: knowing when JavaScript actually breaks for real users, understanding how pages perform for those same real users, and getting lightweight usage analytics without deploying a heavier tracking suite. After adding a single script tag, CatchJS automatically listens for unhandled JavaScript errors across a site and reports them with full stack traces and surrounding context, similar in concept to larger error-tracking platforms but built around a dramatically smaller script footprint, reported at under 1.8KB after compression, which the company markets as up to 20 times lighter than typical competing error trackers.

Alongside error capture, CatchJS records real-user performance metrics tied directly to Core Web Vitals and user experience, including Largest Contentful Paint, Cumulative Layout Shift, and First Input Delay, plus simple, privacy-conscious analytics covering pageviews and time on page. This combination lets a small team get error visibility, performance monitoring, and basic analytics from one lightweight tool instead of running three separate services, which is part of why CatchJS is also distributed as a dedicated Shopify app for store owners who want the same visibility without custom development work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Tiers and Who CatchJS Is Built For
paragraphs: CatchJS's three pricing tiers scale primarily along logged error volume and the number of URLs being monitored for performance and pageviews, rather than along team seats or feature gating, which keeps the plans simple to reason about. The Development plan (from $44/month annually) fits small sites, staging environments, or early-stage products with modest error and traffic volume, covering 1,000 logged errors and 100 monitored URLs per month. The Production plan (from $79/month annually) is aimed at live, revenue-generating websites with higher error and traffic volume, offering 100x the error and pageview allowances of the Development tier. The Enterprise plan (from $449/month annually) is built for high-traffic applications or agencies managing many domains, supporting up to a million logged errors a month and coverage across 25 domains with unlimited subdomains.

Given its small script size and simple setup, CatchJS tends to suit small-to-midsize engineering teams, indie developers, and Shopify merchants who want error visibility and basic performance monitoring without the cost, complexity, or page-weight overhead of enterprise observability platforms like Sentry or Datadog. Its Norway-based, apparently small and independently run team also suggests a product built for a focused niche, script-weight-conscious error tracking, rather than an attempt to compete head-on with full-scale application performance monitoring suites.

enrichment.short_description: cdnjs is a free, open-source content delivery network for JavaScript and CSS libraries, used by developers to load popular front-end libraries quickly and reliably without hosting the files themselves. It was created in January 2011 by Ryan Kirkman and Thomas Davis, launched publicly on GitHub in February 2011, and partnered with Cloudflare in June 2011 for global CDN infrastructure and the cdnjs.cloudflare.com subdomain; on November 1, 2019, the original founders formally transferred full ownership and operation of the project to Cloudflare, citing technical and commercial reasons. Cloudflare has continued running cdnjs as a free service ever since, distributing thousands of open-source JavaScript and CSS libraries (over 4,000 as of the last publicly cited count, with the number growing continuously as maintainers publish updates) stored publicly on GitHub, and serving reportedly over 200 billion requests per month across more than 12 percent of all websites on the internet. Because it is free, open-source, and community-maintained with Cloudflare providing the underlying infrastructure, cdnjs has no pricing plans, subscription tiers, or company sales process; developers simply link to a library's hosted file URL to include it in a project, and Cloudflare's global edge network handles delivery and caching automatically.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Everything to know about cdnjs, the free open-source CDN for JavaScript and CSS libraries run by Cloudflare: history, features, usage, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: cdnjs is a free, open-source content delivery network purpose-built for serving popular JavaScript and CSS libraries to websites around the world, letting developers include a library in their project by linking to a hosted file URL instead of downloading, bundling, and self-hosting it. The project began in January 2011 when developers Ryan Kirkman and Thomas Davis created it, publishing the code publicly on GitHub on February 25, 2011. Just a few months later, on June 15, 2011, cdnjs partnered with Cloudflare, which provided the underlying global CDN infrastructure and the cdnjs.cloudflare.com subdomain that developers still use to reference hosted libraries today. That partnership deepened over the following years until November 1, 2019, when Kirkman and Davis formally transferred full ownership and operational control of cdnjs to Cloudflare, stating the move was made for technical and commercial reasons as the project's scale had grown well beyond what a small volunteer team could sustainably operate. Since that transfer, Cloudflare has continued running cdnjs as a free public service, funding its infrastructure, security review processes, and automation for pulling in new library versions, while the actual list of hosted libraries continues to be maintained through open contributions on GitHub, keeping the project open source in spirit even though a single company now operates it. At the scale it operates today, cdnjs has been reported to serve over 200 billion requests every month and to be used on more than 12 percent of all websites globally, reflecting how deeply embedded it has become in the everyday web development workflow, particularly for teams that want to quickly prototype using well-known libraries like jQuery, Bootstrap, Font Awesome, or Animate.css without setting up a build pipeline or package manager step. As of the last broadly cited figures, cdnjs hosted more than 4,000 distinct JavaScript and CSS libraries, though that number has continued to grow as new libraries are added and maintained by the open-source community feeding the project's GitHub repository; developers can browse the full library catalog and copy ready-to-use script or link tags directly from cdnjs.com. Because cdnjs is fully free, open source, and operated by Cloudflare at no direct cost to end users, it has no pricing plans, subscription tiers, paid feature gates, or sales process of any kind; there is nothing to purchase or subscribe to, and using cdnjs simply means pointing an HTML script or link tag at a hosted file URL, after which Cloudflare's globally distributed edge network handles caching and delivery automatically. This makes cdnjs functionally comparable to other free open-source CDNs like jsDelivr and unpkg, though its long operating history since 2011 and direct backing by Cloudflare's infrastructure have made it one of the most widely used and trusted options in that category.

long_form_blocks.heading: History and Ownership Transition to Cloudflare
paragraphs: cdnjs began as a side project in January 2011, created by developers Ryan Kirkman and Thomas Davis who wanted an easy way for the web development community to load common JavaScript and CSS libraries from a fast, reliable, centrally hosted location instead of everyone self-hosting the same files repeatedly. The project's code was published publicly on GitHub on February 25, 2011, inviting community contributions almost from the start, which is part of why its library catalog grew so quickly in its early years.

Just months after launch, on June 15, 2011, cdnjs entered a partnership with Cloudflare, which provided the global CDN infrastructure needed to actually serve files quickly around the world, along with the now-familiar cdnjs.cloudflare.com subdomain. That partnership continued for roughly eight years until November 1, 2019, when Kirkman and Davis formally handed full ownership and day-to-day operation of cdnjs over to Cloudflare entirely, citing technical and commercial reasons tied to the project's scale. Since then, Cloudflare has run cdnjs as a fully free public service while the project's library list continues to be maintained through open, community-driven contributions on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Developers Actually Use cdnjs
paragraphs: In practice, using cdnjs requires no signup, account, or configuration: a developer browses or searches the library catalog at cdnjs.com, copies the provided script or link tag for the specific library and version they need, and pastes it into their HTML. From that point forward, Cloudflare's globally distributed edge network handles caching and delivering that file to visitors from a nearby location, which typically improves load times compared to hosting the file on a single origin server, and it also means popular libraries are often already cached in a visitor's browser from having loaded the same file on another site using cdnjs.

Because the service is free, open source, and backed by Cloudflare's infrastructure rather than a monetized product with usage tiers, cdnjs is most commonly used for quick prototyping, learning projects, smaller websites without a modern build pipeline, and situations where a team wants to avoid bundling a large dependency into their own JavaScript build. Larger, more complex applications increasingly rely on npm-based bundlers instead, but cdnjs remains a staple reference point across tutorials, code examples (such as on CodePen or JSFiddle), and any project that wants to add a well-known library with a single line of HTML.

enrichment.short_description: cDox is a privacy-first document and spreadsheet collaboration platform positioned as a Google Docs and Microsoft 365 alternative for users who want their data kept out of AI training pipelines and hosted under data-sovereign legal jurisdiction. Built by BFH Labs Inc., a Canadian company, cDox currently hosts all user data on independent bare-metal servers physically located in Montreal, Quebec, governed by Canadian and Quebec law, with plans to expand hosting to additional countries, including France, Germany, the UK, Switzerland, and Australia, through 2027, so users will eventually be able to choose which country's jurisdiction their data lives under. The platform combines word-processing (pages) and spreadsheet (sheets) functionality with real-time collaboration via email invitations, auto-saving, one-click publishing to shareable public links, export options to Markdown, PDF, Word, and CSV, plus features like dark mode, keyboard shortcuts, threaded comments, and REST API access. Its core promise, stated explicitly by the company, is that user content is never used to train AI models and is never extracted or mined for other purposes. cDox targets writers, journalists, researchers, developers, editors, therapists, healthcare providers, nonprofits, and educators, groups for whom data privacy and jurisdictional control matter more than the deepest possible feature set. Pricing is freemium: a Free tier offers 50MB of storage and up to 3 public links, while the Pro tier costs $8 per month or $70 per year and unlocks 500MB of storage and unlimited public links.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: What is cDox? A look at the Canadian, privacy-first document and spreadsheet platform: features, pricing, data sovereignty model, and who it is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: cDox is a document and spreadsheet collaboration platform built around the core idea of data sovereignty and privacy, positioning itself as an alternative to Google Docs, Google Sheets, and Microsoft 365 for individuals and organizations that specifically do not want their documents used to train AI models or hosted under jurisdictions and legal frameworks they cannot control. The product is made by BFH Labs Inc., a Canadian company, and the founder has publicly framed the project's mission around what the company describes as de-big-teching, an approach rooted in open web principles that pushes back against large technology platforms mining user content for AI training or advertising purposes. At present, cDox hosts all user data exclusively on independent bare-metal servers physically located in Montreal, Quebec, meaning the data is governed under Canadian and Quebec law rather than the jurisdiction of a large multinational cloud provider. The company has stated plans to expand its hosting footprint to additional countries, including France, Germany, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, and Australia, through 2027, with the eventual goal of letting users choose which country's servers and legal jurisdiction their documents are stored under, a feature not commonly offered by mainstream productivity suites. Functionally, cDox combines two core document types: pages, a word-processing style document for long-form writing, and sheets, a spreadsheet tool for structured or tabular data, both supporting real-time collaboration where users invite collaborators by email and see edits sync live. Documents auto-save continuously to the underlying bare-metal infrastructure, and users can publish any document with one click to generate a shareable public link, or export it in common formats including Markdown, PDF, Microsoft Word, CSV, and plain text, ensuring content is not locked into a proprietary format. Additional features include a dark mode interface, keyboard shortcuts for power users, threaded commenting for collaborative feedback, and REST API access for developers who want to integrate cDox documents into other tools or workflows. The company's most emphasized commitment is that user content is never used to train AI models and is never extracted or mined by the company for any secondary purpose, a direct point of differentiation from major productivity suites that have faced scrutiny over how user content may be used in relation to AI training. Given this positioning, cDox's stated target audience includes writers, journalists, researchers, developers, editors, therapists, healthcare providers, nonprofits, and educators, professions and organizations where confidentiality, source protection, client privacy, or simply a philosophical preference against feeding personal or professional writing into AI training pipelines carries real weight. On pricing, cDox follows a freemium subscription model: the Free tier includes 50MB of storage and up to 3 public shareable links, sufficient for casual or exploratory use, while the paid Pro tier costs $8 per month or $70 per year (a modest discount for annual billing) and unlocks 500MB of storage along with unlimited public links, aimed at more active writers, small teams, or organizations that need more capacity and unrestricted sharing. As a relatively young, early-stage company without extensive public financial or team-size disclosures, prospective users evaluating cDox for sensitive or regulated use cases, such as healthcare or legal documentation, should independently verify its current compliance posture and security practices rather than assuming parity with larger, more established enterprise productivity platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Data Sovereignty Model at the Core of cDox
paragraphs: What sets cDox apart from mainstream productivity suites like Google Docs or Microsoft 365 is not a novel editing feature but a fundamentally different stance on where and under what legal jurisdiction user data lives, and what it can be used for. Rather than hosting documents in a large multinational cloud provider's data centers subject to that provider's terms of service and jurisdiction, cDox currently stores all content on independent bare-metal servers physically located in Montreal, Quebec, placing user data explicitly under Canadian and Quebec law. The company has publicly committed that user content is never used to train AI models and is never extracted or mined for any secondary commercial purpose, a direct response to growing concern among writers, journalists, researchers, and privacy-conscious professionals about how their work might otherwise be used by large AI-focused technology companies.

Beyond its current Montreal hosting, cDox has stated plans to expand its infrastructure to additional countries, including France, Germany, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, and Australia, through 2027, with the long-term goal of letting users actively choose which country's servers and legal framework their documents are stored under. This kind of user-selectable data residency is uncommon even among enterprise-grade productivity tools, and reflects cDox's positioning toward users and organizations for whom jurisdictional control over sensitive documents is a genuine requirement rather than a nice-to-have.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Features and Who cDox Is Built For
paragraphs: Functionally, cDox provides the two document types most users need day to day: pages for word-processing style long-form writing, and sheets for spreadsheet-style structured data, both with real-time collaborative editing via email-based invitations. Documents auto-save continuously, can be published with one click to a shareable public link, and can be exported into Markdown, PDF, Word, or CSV formats so users are never locked into a proprietary file format. Additional touches like dark mode, keyboard shortcuts, threaded comments, and REST API access round out the feature set for both casual writers and more technical users who want to integrate cDox into their own workflows.

Given its privacy-first and data-sovereignty positioning, cDox's stated target users skew toward professions where confidentiality and control over content genuinely matter: writers and journalists concerned about source protection or having their unpublished drafts feed AI training data, researchers and academics handling sensitive study material, therapists and healthcare providers with client confidentiality obligations, and nonprofits and educators who prefer to avoid dependence on large technology platforms. For general-purpose office productivity without those specific privacy priorities, mainstream tools like Google Docs or Microsoft 365 will typically still offer a broader and more mature feature set; cDox's value proposition is specifically about trading some of that breadth for jurisdictional control and an explicit no-AI-training commitment.
